reflection: What ending can you view with a smile of gratitude?
author: Gabriel García Márquez

title: Paradise
body: I have always imagined that Paradise will be a kind of library.
reflection: Where is your paradise?
author: Jorge Luis Borges

title: Good enough
body: Good enough is often better than perfect, and almost always better than never.
reflection: Where are you waiting for perfect instead of starting?

title: The middle years
body: There's a particular clarity that comes in the middle of life. You've lived enough to know what matters.
reflection: What do you know now that you couldn't have known at twenty?

title: Routines
body: Most of life is routine. The question is whether yours are ones you chose.
reflection: Which of your daily habits still serve you?

title: Unfinished things
body: Some things were never meant to be finished. They were meant to be lived.
reflection: What have you been putting off that might be better left imperfect?

title: Small pleasures
body: The small pleasures don't feel small when you're really paying attention.
reflection: What small pleasure did you overlook today?

title: Sunday afternoons
body: There used to be an art to doing nothing on a Sunday afternoon. It may be worth recovering.
reflection: When did you last spend an afternoon with no agenda?

title: Old photographs
body: Old photographs remind you that the ordinary was always extraordinary.
reflection: What ordinary moment from your past would you return to?

title: Changing your mind
body: Changing your mind is not weakness. It means you learned something.
reflection: What have you changed your mind about in the last few years?

title: The body
body: The body keeps score whether you pay attention to it or not. Better to pay attention.
reflection: What is your body telling you right now that you've been ignoring?

title: Keeping score
body: At some point you realize nobody's keeping score but you.
reflection: What score are you tracking that no longer means anything?

title: Inherited habits
body: Some of our habits weren't chosen. They were handed down. Worth looking at which ones you'd actually keep.
reflection: Which habits did you inherit that you'd choose again? Which would you let go?

title: Asking for help
body: Asking for help is not a sign that you've failed. It's a sign that you understand your limits.
reflection: What have you been carrying alone that you could ask for help with?

title: What you notice
body: What you pay attention to shapes your life more than most of the decisions you make.
reflection: What have you been paying attention to that isn't serving you?

title: Growing up
body: Growing up doesn't happen all at once. It happens in small, unremarkable moments.
reflection: When did you feel yourself genuinely grow?

title: What remains
body: When the noise settles, what remains is usually what matters.
reflection: What is left when you remove the noise from your day?

title: Apologies
body: A late apology is almost always better than none at all.
reflection: Is there an apology you've been putting off?

title: Reading the room
body: Some rooms take years to understand. Some you never quite figure out.
reflection: What situation in your life are you still trying to read?

title: Not knowing
body: Not knowing is uncomfortable. It's also where most of the interesting things are.
reflection: What uncertainty in your life might actually be an opening?

title: The long view
body: From far enough away, most of your problems look different. Not smaller, just different.
reflection: What problem looks different when you take the long view?

title: Gratitude
body: Gratitude is easy to say and hard to feel. The feeling is worth working toward.
reflection: What do you have right now that you once badly wanted?

title: Holding on
body: Sometimes holding on takes more out of you than letting go would.
reflection: What are you holding on to that has already ended?

title: What people remember
body: People rarely remember what you accomplished. They remember how you treated them.
reflection: How do the people closest to you experience you?

title: The gap
body: The gap between who you are and who you want to be is not a failure. It's a direction.
reflection: What one step would close the gap a little?

title: Contentment
body: Contentment isn't the same as settling. It's the ability to be where you are.
reflection: What would it feel like to be fully content with today?

title: Saying no
body: Every yes is a no to something else. The question is which something else.
reflection: What did you say yes to recently that was actually a no to something more important?

title: After the fact
body: Most wisdom arrives after the fact. The trick is letting it arrive at all.
reflection: What lesson arrived late that you're still integrating?

title: The hard years
body: The hard years teach things the easy ones never could. That doesn't make them easier.
reflection: What did a difficult period teach you that you wouldn't trade?

title: Home
body: Home is less a place and more a feeling of being known.
reflection: Where do you feel most known?

title: Kindness
body: Kindness costs less than almost anything and buys more than most things.
reflection: Where could you offer a little more kindness today?

title: Moving on
body: Moving on doesn't mean forgetting. It means the past has found its right size.
reflection: What part of your past is still too large?

title: The present
body: The present moment is the only one you can actually do anything in.
reflection: What would change if you fully arrived in today?

title: What's left unsaid
body: The things left unsaid have a way of sitting heavily.
reflection: What have you been meaning to say?

title: Milestones
body: Most milestones aren't announced. You only recognize them looking back.
reflection: What milestone passed quietly that deserves acknowledgment?

title: Forgiveness
body: Forgiveness doesn't excuse anything. It just stops the past from steering.
reflection: What would you be free to do if you put down a particular grievance?

title: Being wrong
body: Being wrong is uncomfortable. Being unable to admit it is far more costly.
reflection: Where might you currently be wrong?

title: Evening light
body: There is a particular kind of peace in the light at the end of a day.
reflection: When did you last stop to notice it?

title: What endures
body: What endures from a life is rarely what you planned.
reflection: What part of your life do you hope endures?

title: Neighbors
body: The people closest in proximity are often the least known.
reflection: Who lives near you that you've never really spoken to?

title: Honesty
body: Honesty is easier in the long run. The short run is what makes it hard.
reflection: Where are you choosing comfort over honesty right now?

title: Enough time
body: There is rarely enough time and almost always more than you think.
reflection: What have you convinced yourself there's no time for?

title: Second chances
body: Second chances are rarer than people think, and more common than they deserve.
reflection: What second chance have you been given that you've used well?

title: Listening
body: Real listening is rarer than it should be. When someone offers it, you feel it.
reflection: Who in your life listens to you in a way that feels real?

title: The body knows
body: The body often knows something is wrong before the mind admits it.
reflection: What has your body been signaling that you've been rationalizing away?

title: Starting over
body: Starting over at any age is harder than it sounds and better than the alternative.
reflection: What would you start over on, if you believed it was possible?

title: What you model
body: Children notice less what you say and more what you do. So does everyone else.
reflection: What are you modeling that you'd want to change?

title: The shape of a day
body: The shape of your days eventually becomes the shape of your life.
reflection: Does the shape of your days match the life you want?

title: Getting older
body: Getting older is not the problem. Resisting it is where the suffering comes from.
reflection: What part of getting older have you been resisting?

title: What you carry
body: You can set things down. Most people just don’t realize they can.
reflection: What have you been carrying that you have permission to put down?

title: Simple meals
body: Some of the most memorable meals were simple. The company made them.
reflection: Who would you most like to share a meal with right now?

title: Trust
body: Trust takes a long time to build and almost no time to break. It’s worth protecting.
reflection: Where in your life could you extend a little more trust?

title: Regret
body: Regret is information, not punishment. The question is what you do with it.
reflection: What does your regret tell you about what you value?

title: When it clicks
body: Some things click only after you’ve stopped forcing them.
reflection: What have you been forcing that might benefit from some release?

title: Joy
body: Joy is quieter than happiness and lasts longer.
reflection: When did you last feel something you'd call real joy?

title: The long friendship
body: A long friendship is one of the rarest and most underrated things a life can contain.
reflection: Who is your longest friendship with, and when did you last honor it?

title: What you owe yourself
body: You owe yourself at least the same consideration you give most other people.
reflection: Where are you giving others what you won't give yourself?

title: Repair
body: Most things that break can be repaired. Most people wait too long to try.
reflection: What in your life is broken and still repairable?

title: The afternoon
body: There’s a kind of freedom in a slow afternoon that can’t be manufactured.
reflection: When did you last have a truly unscheduled afternoon?

title: Old books
body: An old book with someone else’s notes in the margin is a conversation across time.
reflection: What book marked you, and what would you write back to it now?

title: Endings
body: Endings are rarely as final as they feel.
reflection: What ending in your life turned out to be a beginning?

title: What matters
body: What matters to you now probably isn’t what mattered to you at thirty. That’s not inconsistency. That’s growth.
reflection: What mattered to you then that no longer does?

title: Patience
body: Patience isn’t waiting. It’s knowing how to be while you wait.
reflection: What are you waiting for, and how are you being in the meantime?

title: Old neighborhoods
body: Old neighborhoods hold a version of you that the present one has lost track of.
reflection: What place from your past still carries a part of you?

title: Telling the truth
body: Telling the truth gets easier the more you practice it.
reflection: Where have you been less than honest with someone who deserves more?

title: The feeling of enough
body: Enough is not a number. It’s a feeling. Most people never locate it.
reflection: Have you ever felt it? What were the conditions?

title: Laughter
body: Laughter at the right moment is one of the best things two people can share.
reflection: Who makes you laugh in a way that’s hard to explain?

title: Decisions
body: Most decisions are reversible. Treating them as if they’re not costs more than the decision itself.
reflection: What decision have you been treating as permanent that might not be?

title: The questions that stay
body: Some questions don’t get answered. They just get lived with more comfortably over time.
reflection: What question have you been living with for a long time?

title: What the body remembers
body: The body holds memories the mind has released. Sometimes they surface without warning.
reflection: What does your body remember that your mind has let go?

title: Difficult people
body: Difficult people are often carrying something you can’t see.
reflection: Who in your life might deserve more patience than you’ve been giving?

title: Midlife
body: Midlife isn’t a crisis. It’s a question. The crisis is refusing to answer it.
reflection: What question is midlife asking you right now?

title: What you built
body: Look at what you’ve built over the years. Even imperfect things took effort worth acknowledging.
reflection: What have you built, quietly, that you’re proud of?

title: The ordinary Tuesday
body: A Tuesday in the middle of a good life is worth more than you give it credit for.
reflection: What is good about today, right now, that you haven’t noticed?

title: Making peace
body: Making peace with the past doesn’t require agreeing with it.
reflection: What part of your past are you still at war with?

title: Doing it anyway
body: Doing something while afraid is not the same as not being afraid. It’s often braver.
reflection: What are you doing anyway, despite the fear?

title: The people who shaped you
body: You carry traces of everyone who ever genuinely loved you.
reflection: Who shaped you in ways you’re still discovering?

title: What’s worth fighting for
body: Some things are worth fighting for. Fewer than most people think.
reflection: What are you currently fighting for? Is it worth it?

title: Letting people in
body: Letting people in is a risk. Keeping everyone out is a different and larger one.
reflection: Who have you been keeping at a distance that you might let a little closer?

title: Changing
body: You are not the same person you were ten years ago. Neither is anyone else.
reflection: How are you different in ways you’re glad about?

title: The small kindnesses
body: The small kindnesses are the ones people actually remember.
reflection: What small kindness has stayed with you from someone else?

title: What it means to rest
body: Rest is not laziness. It is the thing that makes everything else sustainable.
reflection: What does real rest look like for you?

title: The unlived lives
body: Most people mourn unlived lives at some point. What matters is which lives you choose now.
reflection: What version of your life is still available to you?

title: Courage
body: Courage is less dramatic than people think. It’s mostly just showing up when you’d rather not.
reflection: Where are you showing up lately when it would be easier not to?

title: What age teaches
body: Age teaches some things gently and others by force. Both kinds of lessons stick.
reflection: What has age taught you by force that you’ve come to accept?

title: The things we avoid
body: The things we avoid most tend to be the things most worth facing.
reflection: What are you currently avoiding?

title: Becoming
body: You are always in the process of becoming something. The question is whether you’re paying attention to it.
reflection: What are you becoming right now?

title: Old age
body: Old age is not the opposite of youth. It is a different country with its own gifts.
reflection: What gifts do you see in the season you are in?

title: The right amount of alone
body: Finding the right amount of alone is one of the most personal of all equations.
reflection: Do you have enough solitude in your life, or too much?

title: Curiosity
body: Curiosity is one of the best things you can bring to a life. It keeps it open.
reflection: What are you curious about lately?

title: What doesn’t last
body: Not everything is meant to last. Some things are meant to be exactly what they were.
reflection: What in your life was exactly what it was meant to be, even if it ended?

title: Starting
body: Starting is often harder than continuing. Which is why starting, once done, is worth protecting.
reflection: What have you started that deserves protection?

title: Life’s weight
body: Life has weight. Learning to carry it well is not a skill anyone teaches you.
reflection: How have you learned to carry what’s heavy?

title: Honest living
body: An honest life is quieter than a performed one. Also lighter.
reflection: Where are you performing rather than living?

title: What children know
body: Children know things adults have learned to unknow. Some of it is worth recovering.
reflection: What did you know as a child that you’ve lost track of?

title: The list
body: There’s a difference between a full life and a busy one.
reflection: Is your life full, or just full of things to do?

title: Heritage
body: You didn’t choose where you started. You have more say than you think in where you end up.
reflection: What have you made of what you were given?

title: Paying attention
body: A life spent paying attention to the right things is a life well spent.
reflection: What deserves more of your attention?

title: The hard conversations
body: Hard conversations postponed rarely get easier. They just accumulate interest.
reflection: What conversation have you been postponing?

title: Showing up
body: Half of the work is simply showing up, consistently, when it matters.
reflection: Where are you showing up consistently that deserves acknowledgment?

title: Being known
body: Being fully known by even one other person is one of life’s quiet graces.
reflection: Who really knows you? Do they know that you feel that way?

title: On dignity
body: Dignity is not something you claim. It’s how you carry yourself when no one is watching.
reflection: How do you carry yourself when no one is watching?

title: The window
body: The view from a window changes depending on the season, the light, and where you are in your life.
reflection: What does the view from your window tell you about where you are?

title: Meaning
body: Meaning isn’t found. It’s built, slowly, from what you give yourself to.
reflection: What are you giving yourself to that builds meaning?

title: The years
body: The years feel long and short at the same time. Both are true.
reflection: What do you want to be true of this particular year?

title: What you model
body: What you do daily is what you believe. Everything else is just what you say.
reflection: What do your daily actions say about what you believe?

title: Belonging
body: Belonging is not about being like everyone else. It’s about being accepted as you are.
reflection: Where do you belong in that way?

title: The pace of things
body: Life doesn’t have to be lived at the pace the world sets.
reflection: What pace would you choose if you actually had the choice?

title: Accepting help
body: Accepting help gracefully is its own kind of skill.
reflection: Is there help available to you right now that you’ve been refusing?

title: The ones who stayed
body: The ones who stayed when it was hard are worth more than you’ve probably told them.
reflection: Who stayed for you? Have you told them what that meant?

title: What gets left behind
body: What you leave behind is shaped by what you paid attention to while you were here.
reflection: What would you like to leave behind?

title: The first step
body: The first step is rarely the hardest. The step after a long pause is.
reflection: What step are you taking after a pause?

title: On being human
body: Being human means making mistakes, carrying them, and sometimes managing to set them down.
reflection: What mistake are you still carrying that might be time to set down?

title: What you are for
body: Knowing what you’re for is more useful than knowing what you’re against.
reflection: What are you for?

title: The afternoon light
body: Late afternoon light has a warmth that morning light doesn’t. Neither is better. Both are worth stopping for.
reflection: When did you last stop for the light?

title: Making things
body: Making something with your hands returns you to yourself.
reflection: What have you made recently, and how did it feel?

title: The habit of noticing
body: Noticing is a habit. Like most habits, it strengthens with practice.
reflection: What have you been noticing lately?

title: Living forward
body: Life is understood backward but must be lived forward. The confusion this creates is normal.
reflection: What in your past makes more sense to you now than it did then?
author: Søren Kierkegaard

title: On courage
body: You gain strength, courage, and confidence by every experience in which you really stop to look fear in the face.
reflection: What fear have you recently looked in the face?
author: Eleanor Roosevelt

title: On growth
body: We cannot become what we want by remaining what we are.
reflection: What would you need to let go of to grow into who you want to be?
author: Max DePree

title: The examined life
body: He who has a why to live can bear almost any how.
reflection: What is your why right now?
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

title: On living fully
body: It is not length of life, but depth of life.
reflection: Where could you go deeper rather than faster?
author: Ralph Waldo Emerson

title: On possibility
body: In the middle of difficulty lies opportunity.
reflection: What opportunity might be hidden in your current difficulty?
author: Albert Einstein

title: On character
body: Character is what you are in the dark.
reflection: Who are you when no one is watching?
author: Dwight L. Moody

title: The uses of adversity
body: Sweet are the uses of adversity, which, like the toad, ugly and venomous, wears yet a precious jewel in his head.
reflection: What jewel has your adversity revealed?
author: William Shakespeare

title: On living
body: To live is the rarest thing in the world. Most people exist, that is all.
reflection: What is the difference between existing and living for you right now?
author: Oscar Wilde

title: Being alone
body: Being alone is different from being lonely. One is a choice; the other is a feeling.
reflection: Which are you experiencing more of right now?

title: The quiet house
body: A quiet house has its own kind of conversation.
reflection: What does the quiet of your home say to you?

title: Inner noise
body: Some of the loudest noise is the kind only you can hear.
reflection: What is the loudest noise inside you right now?

title: Solitude and self
body: Solitude is where you find out what you actually think, as opposed to what you’ve been told to think.
reflection: What do you actually think, when no one else is in the room?

title: The walk alone
body: A long walk taken alone can settle more than a long conversation.
reflection: When did you last walk without a destination or a phone?

title: Silence
body: Silence is not empty. It’s full of what you’ve been too busy to hear.
reflection: What have you been too busy to hear?

title: The self you return to
body: No matter how much the world changes, there is a self you return to when everything quiets.
reflection: What is that self like?

title: Aloneness
body: Aloneness is a prerequisite for self-knowledge. You can’t know yourself in a crowd.
reflection: How well do you know yourself right now?

title: The inner life
body: An inner life is not given. It is cultivated, in private, over many years.
reflection: What does your inner life look like right now?

title: Saturday mornings alone
body: A morning spent alone without obligation is a rare gift worth protecting.
reflection: When did you last have a morning with no obligation?

title: Reading alone
body: Reading alone in a quiet room is one of the oldest and best ways to spend time.
reflection: What are you reading, or what would you like to be?

title: The undistracted hour
body: An hour without distraction reveals things an hour of noise never could.
reflection: What would an undistracted hour show you?

title: Sitting still
body: Sitting still is harder than it sounds and more useful than it looks.
reflection: When did you last sit still without reaching for something?

title: What you think about
body: What you think about when no one is asking says a great deal about who you are.
reflection: What do you find yourself thinking about in unguarded moments?

title: The long evening
body: There is a kind of richness in a long, unrushed evening alone.
reflection: When did you last give yourself an unrushed evening?

title: Self-reliance
body: Learning to enjoy your own company is one of the more underrated life skills.
reflection: Do you enjoy your own company? What makes it easier or harder?

title: Withdrawal
body: Withdrawal is not retreat. Sometimes it’s the most productive thing available.
reflection: What would you gain from stepping back right now?

title: Thinking things through
body: Some problems untangle themselves when you stop pulling and just sit with them.
reflection: What problem might benefit from some quiet sitting?

title: The company of books
body: Books are some of the best company. They ask nothing of you except attention.
reflection: What book has been good company to you?

title: Just being
body: Just being, without producing or performing, is harder than it should be.
reflection: When did you last just be?

title: What silence holds
body: Silence holds things that conversation crowds out.
reflection: What has silence held for you lately?

title: The private life
body: Everyone has a private life. The question is whether you’ve tended to yours.
reflection: Have you tended to your private life lately?

title: The thinking mind
body: The thinking mind needs space. It doesn’t work well when constantly crowded.
reflection: How much space are you giving your mind?

title: The morning before others wake
body: The hour before others wake is one of the most valuable of the day.
reflection: What do you do with your earliest waking hour?

title: Sitting with discomfort
body: Sitting with discomfort instead of running from it is harder than almost anything. Also more instructive.
reflection: What discomfort are you running from right now?

title: The room of one’s own
body: Everyone needs a place to think. Physical or otherwise.
reflection: Where do you go to think?

title: Boredom
body: Boredom has gotten a bad reputation. It’s often where the most interesting thoughts begin.
reflection: When did you last let yourself be bored?

title: What returns in quiet
body: In quiet, things return that the noise had chased away.
reflection: What has returned to you in a quiet moment recently?

title: Introversion
body: Needing time alone to recover isn’t a flaw. It’s information about how you’re built.
reflection: Are you giving yourself the recovery time you actually need?

title: The night hours
body: The night has a different quality to it. Thoughts arrive that the daylight keeps at bay.
reflection: What thoughts arrive for you at night?

title: Unstructured time
body: Unstructured time feels uncomfortable at first. Then it feels like the most natural thing in the world.
reflection: What would you do with a full unstructured day?

title: Being with yourself
body: The longest relationship you’ll ever have is with yourself. It’s worth investing in.
reflection: What would investing in that relationship look like right now?

title: The sound of your own thoughts
body: You know your own thoughts by sound. Learning to like them is a different matter.
reflection: What do you think of your own thoughts?

title: Contemplation
body: Contemplation is not idle. It is some of the most useful work available.
reflection: What are you contemplating right now?

title: The quiet corner
body: Every life needs a quiet corner. Not everyone has one. It’s worth looking for.
reflection: Do you have a quiet corner? Where is it?

title: Reflective time
body: Without time for reflection, experience is just events. Reflection is what turns them into wisdom.
reflection: What recent experience hasn’t been fully reflected on yet?

title: The absence of others
body: The absence of others is not always a loss. Sometimes it’s a spaciousness.
reflection: What spaciousness has solitude given you recently?

title: Sitting with your life
body: Sitting with your life as it actually is takes more courage than improving it.
reflection: Can you sit with your life as it is right now?

title: Daydreaming
body: Daydreaming is not a waste of time. It’s how the imagination restores itself.
reflection: What do you daydream about?

title: The value of nothing
body: Doing nothing is undervalued because it produces nothing visible. Its products are invisible and lasting.
reflection: What invisible thing did a period of doing nothing produce in you?

title: Checking in
body: Checking in with yourself regularly is maintenance, not indulgence.
reflection: When did you last check in with yourself honestly?

title: Without an audience
body: Who are you without an audience? That version is worth knowing.
reflection: Who are you when no one is watching?

title: The unwitnessed moment
body: Some of the most real moments of a life are unwitnessed.
reflection: What unwitnessed moment has mattered most to you?

title: Loneliness
body: Loneliness and solitude are neighbors with very different temperaments.
reflection: Which are you visiting more right now?

title: Resting the mind
body: The mind needs rest the same way the body does. The consequences of ignoring both are similar.
reflection: When did you last genuinely rest your mind?

title: Yourself as company
body: Yourself, as company, improves with attention paid.
reflection: What would you need to attend to in order to be better company for yourself?

title: The daily check-in
body: A few minutes of honest quiet each day can prevent months of confusion later.
reflection: What would you discover in a few minutes of honest quiet today?

title: What you know alone
body: There are things you only know alone. They’re some of the most important things you know.
reflection: What do you know about yourself that you’ve only ever discovered in solitude?

title: On knowing oneself
body: To know what you prefer, instead of humbly saying Amen to what the world tells you you ought to prefer, is to keep your soul alive.
reflection: What do you prefer, without needing anyone else to confirm it?
author: Robert Louis Stevenson

title: On inner wealth
body: The person who follows the crowd will usually go no further than the crowd. The person who walks alone is likely to find themselves in places no one has ever been.
reflection: Where has walking alone taken you?
author: Albert Einstein

title: The inner voice
body: The quieter you become, the more you are able to hear.
reflection: What are you hearing in the quiet?
author: Ram Dass

title: On withdrawing
body: In the rush of modern life, sometimes the bravest thing is to stop.
reflection: What would stopping make possible for you?

title: The still small voice
body: After the earthquake a fire; and after the fire a still small voice.
reflection: When did you last hear that still small voice?
author: 1 Kings 19:12

title: On depths
body: I never found a companion that was so companionable as solitude.
reflection: What has solitude offered you that company couldn’t?
author: Henry David Thoreau

title: On emptiness
body: Empty yourself of everything. Let the mind rest at peace.
reflection: What are you holding that you could empty out?
author: Laozi

title: On silence II
body: Everything that is made beautiful and fair and lovely is made for the eye of one who sees.
reflection: What beauty are you alone in seeing?
author: Rumi

title: The examined quiet
body: Without great solitude, no serious work is possible.
reflection: What serious work is waiting for you to get quiet?
author: Pablo Picasso

title: On being still
body: Be still and know.
reflection: What do you know when you are still?
author: Psalm 46:10

title: On the self
body: The greatest thing in the world is to know how to belong to oneself.
reflection: How well do you belong to yourself right now?
author: Michel de Montaigne

title: On restoration
body: I need to be alone. I need to ponder my shame and my despair in seclusion.
reflection: What do you need solitude to help you process right now?
author: Henry Miller

title: On the art of being alone
body: Those who know how to be alone know how to be with others.
reflection: How has solitude shaped how you show up with others?

title: On depth
body: In silence, one finds truth and depth.
reflection: What truth is available to you in silence?

title: On renewal
body: Solitude is where I place my chaos to rest and awaken my inner peace.
reflection: What chaos needs resting right now?

title: On the alone time
body: If you are never alone, you cannot know yourself.
reflection: How well have you been knowing yourself lately?
author: Paulo Coelho

title: The mind at rest
body: The mind at rest is not an idle mind. It is a mind doing its most important work.
reflection: When did you last let your mind rest?

title: On being undistracted
body: Almost everything will work again if you unplug it for a few minutes, including you.
reflection: When did you last fully unplug?
author: Anne Lamott

title: On clearing
body: You must have a room, or a certain hour or so a day, where you don’t know what was in the newspapers that morning.
reflection: What is your version of that room?
author: Joseph Campbell

title: The fullness of nothing
body: Nothing is so full as an empty morning.
reflection: What fills your empty mornings?

title: The interior season
body: The inner life has its seasons too. Not all of them are spring.
reflection: What season is your inner life in right now?

title: The private world
body: Everyone carries a private world. Solitude is where you go to visit it.
reflection: When did you last visit yours?

title: Margins
body: A life without margins is a page that’s too full to read.
reflection: Where could you add a margin?

title: Quiet as practice
body: Quiet can be practiced. Like most practices, it gets easier the more you do it.
reflection: How would you begin to practice more quiet?

title: The returning self
body: After noise, the self that returns in quiet is more honest.
reflection: Who returns to you in quiet?

title: Replenishment
body: Some people replenish in company. Others replenish alone. Both are right.
reflection: How do you replenish?

title: On listening to yourself
body: The mind is not a vessel to be filled, but a fire to be kindled.
reflection: What kindles yours when you’re alone?
author: Plutarch

title: On the interior life
body: Your inner life is the most important one. Tend to it.
reflection: How are you tending to it?

title: What the quiet holds
body: The quiet holds what the noise displaces.
reflection: What has the quiet been holding for you?

title: On trusting yourself
body: Trust yourself. You know more than you think you do.
reflection: What do you know, in quiet moments, that you second-guess in company?
author: Benjamin Spock

title: Being with uncertainty
body: Sitting with uncertainty without filling it is one of the harder disciplines.
reflection: What uncertainty are you sitting with right now?

title: The hour of return
body: There is an hour each day when you are most yourself. Worth protecting.
reflection: What hour is that for you?

title: On depth II
body: The more powerful and original a mind, the more it will incline toward the religion of solitude.
reflection: What has your solitude produced that company could not?
author: Aldous Huxley

title: On the threshold
body: In the middle of difficulty, solitude is often a threshold to clarity.
reflection: What clarity has solitude brought you through a difficulty?

title: On the quality of quiet
body: Not all quiet is the same. Some quiet restores. Some just amplifies the noise within.
reflection: What kind of quiet do you have access to right now?

title: On the company of the self
body: The best thinking happens when you’re alone with a problem and no one else is adding their noise.
reflection: What problem deserves some alone time with you?

title: On choosing yourself
body: Solitude is not about being away from people. It is about being with yourself.
reflection: When are you most with yourself?

title: The cleared space
body: A cleared space—mental or physical—invites what needs to arrive.
reflection: What might arrive if you cleared some space?

title: On returning
body: You always return to yourself eventually. The question is in what condition.
reflection: In what condition did you last return to yourself?

title: On the necessary alone
body: There are certain things a person can only work out alone.
reflection: What are you working out right now that requires that kind of solitude?

title: On being unreachable
body: Being unreachable for a little while is not irresponsible. It is often the most responsible thing.
reflection: When did you last allow yourself to be unreachable?

title: On sitting with questions
body: The point is not to answer all the questions. It is to live them.
reflection: What question are you living right now?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

title: Stillness
body: Stillness is not the absence of movement. It is the presence of yourself.
reflection: When do you feel most still?

title: On the inner compass
body: In solitude you know which direction is actually north.
reflection: What direction is actually north for you right now?

title: The unfinished thought
body: Some thoughts need to be left unfinished. They complete themselves later.
reflection: What thought have you left unfinished that might be ready to complete?

title: On inner nourishment
body: The soul needs nourishment that the social world rarely provides.
reflection: What nourishes your soul that only solitude makes possible?

title: On self-understanding
body: There is only one corner of the universe you can be certain of improving, and that is your own self.
reflection: What corner of yourself are you currently improving?
author: Aldous Huxley

title: On patience with yourself
body: Have patience with everything that remains unsolved in your heart.
reflection: What remains unsolved in your heart right now?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

title: On not filling space
body: We need silence to be able to touch souls.
reflection: What soul are you hoping to touch, and does it have access to your silence?
author: Mother Teresa

title: On the gift
body: Loneliness is the poverty of self; solitude is the richness of self.
reflection: Which feels more true for you right now?
author: May Sarton

title: The present hour
body: The present hour is the only one you actually own.
reflection: What are you doing with this one?

title: Where it goes
body: Time doesn't go anywhere. You do.
reflection: Where have you been going lately?

title: Slow time
body: There is a quality to slow time that fast time never has.
reflection: When did you last experience slow time?

title: The morning ritual
body: How you begin a morning sets a tone that follows you.
reflection: What tone does your morning currently set?

title: The last time
body: You rarely know when you're doing something for the last time. That's probably for the best.
reflection: What last time do you wish you'd known about?

title: Lost time
body: Lost time isn't always wasted. Sometimes it was exactly what was needed.
reflection: What period of lost time turned out to matter?

title: The year behind
body: A year contains more than it seems to while you're inside it.
reflection: What did this past year contain that you haven't fully accounted for?

title: Clocks
body: Clocks measure time. They don't tell you what to do with it.
reflection: Are you using time, or is it using you?

title: The right moment
body: The right moment is usually now, disguised as not quite yet.
reflection: What are you waiting for the right moment to do?

title: Time with people
body: Time spent with people you love ages differently than other time.
reflection: Are you spending enough of it?

title: The in-between
body: Life happens as much in the in-between moments as in the ones you plan.
reflection: What happened in the in-between recently?

title: Old age
body: Old age is not a problem to be solved. It is a place to arrive at with some grace.
reflection: How are you preparing for it?

title: What time reveals
body: Time reveals things about people that no amount of observation can.
reflection: What has time revealed to you about someone you care about?

title: The decade
body: A decade is long enough to change almost everything and short enough to surprise you when it ends.
reflection: What did the last decade change?

title: Time and meaning
body: Time without meaning passes, but doesn't accumulate into anything.
reflection: Is your time accumulating into something?

title: What lasts
body: Not everything that lasts is worth keeping. Not everything worth keeping lasts.
reflection: What are you holding that's worth the effort?

title: The pause
body: A pause in the middle of something can be the most useful part of it.
reflection: Where could you use a pause right now?

title: Slow mornings
body: A slow morning, once in a while, is not a luxury. It is a necessity.
reflection: When did you last have one?

title: Time well spent
body: Time well spent rarely looks impressive while it's happening.
reflection: What time have you spent well recently that no one noticed?

title: Deadlines and depth
body: Deadlines produce output. Depth requires something else.
reflection: What in your life requires depth that deadlines can't produce?

title: The years of raising
body: The years of raising children are long in the day and short in retrospect.
reflection: What about those years do you want to remember?

title: Making time
body: You don't find time. You make it. The making is the harder discipline.
reflection: What would you make time for if you believed you could?

title: The years ahead
body: The years ahead are worth thinking about. Not obsessing over. Just thinking about.
reflection: What do you hope for in the years ahead?

title: On timing
body: Good timing is rarely luck. It's usually the result of paying attention for a long time.
reflection: What are you paying close enough attention to?

title: Wasted time
body: Time spent resting is not wasted time. It is invested time.
reflection: Are you resting enough to call it investment?

title: The borrowed hour
body: Every hour is borrowed. The debt is eventual and certain.
reflection: What would you do with your borrowed time today?

title: What age does
body: Age doesn't diminish you. It concentrates you, if you let it.
reflection: What has age concentrated in you?

title: Unhurried
body: An unhurried conversation is increasingly rare. It is worth seeking out.
reflection: When did you last have a conversation that didn't feel rushed?

title: The morning hour
body: The hour before the demands begin is worth protecting.
reflection: What do you do with that hour?

title: Time and perspective
body: A year from now, many things that feel urgent will not be.
reflection: What feels urgent today that probably isn't?

title: On memory
body: Memory is not a recording. It is a story you keep revising.
reflection: What memory have you revised?

title: The long arc
body: The long arc of a life bends in directions you couldn't see from the middle.
reflection: What direction is your arc bending?

title: The right pace
body: Every task has a pace at which it is done best. Rushing is rarely that pace.
reflection: What are you rushing that deserves to be slowed?

title: Time between
body: The time between things is part of the thing.
reflection: What are you doing with the time between?

title: The hour before sleep
body: The hour before sleep is a window. What you put in it shapes what follows.
reflection: What are you putting in that hour?

title: The old photographs
body: Time is not visible until you look at an old photograph.
reflection: What does an old photograph show you about time?

title: What tomorrow holds
body: Tomorrow holds what today couldn't. This is not a reason to rush past today.
reflection: What are you deferring to tomorrow that belongs today?

title: Marking time
body: Rituals mark time in a way that clocks cannot.
reflection: What rituals mark the passage of time for you?

title: The unrecoverable hour
body: Hours are the only currency that cannot be returned once spent.
reflection: How are you spending yours?

title: On impatience
body: Impatience is the cost of wanting the result without the process.
reflection: What are you being impatient about that has a process worth respecting?

title: Evening
body: An unhurried evening is a different experience than a hurried one. Both happen in the same hours.
reflection: How do your evenings tend to feel?

title: The long meal
body: A long, unhurried meal is one of the best uses of two hours.
reflection: When did you last have one?

title: The seasons of work
body: Work has its seasons. The productive ones are sweeter when you've survived the fallow ones.
reflection: What season of work are you in?

title: On growing older
body: Growing older is not a problem to be solved. It is a condition to be inhabited.
reflection: How are you inhabiting it?

title: On remembering
body: The things worth remembering rarely announce themselves as such.
reflection: What from recent weeks might be worth remembering?

title: Saturday
body: Saturday used to feel different. It still can.
reflection: What would make your Saturdays feel different?

title: Arriving late
body: Arriving late to something doesn't mean you missed everything.
reflection: What have you arrived late to that still had something to offer?

title: The unfinished year
body: Every year ends unfinished. The things left undone are not failures. They are continuations.
reflection: What is continuing from last year?

title: On duration
body: The duration of a good thing is not what makes it good.
reflection: What short thing in your life was exactly long enough?

title: On rushing
body: Rushing through good things doesn't get you more of them. It gets you less.
reflection: What good thing are you rushing through?

title: What you do with an afternoon
body: What you do with a free afternoon says something about who you are at the core.
reflection: What do you do with a free afternoon?

title: The count
body: Counting days makes them feel shorter. Making them count makes them feel longer.
reflection: What would make today count?

title: Time and love
body: Love expressed in time is more convincing than love expressed in words.
reflection: How are you spending time with the people you love?

title: On the irreversible
body: Some things, once done or said, cannot be undone. That's worth remembering before, not after.
reflection: What are you about to do or say that deserves that kind of weight?

title: What the future holds
body: The future holds things you can't anticipate. Some of them are better than anything you'd plan.
reflection: What unexpected good has arrived in your life?

title: On present tense
body: Living in present tense is harder than it sounds. It requires giving up several comfortable fictions.
reflection: What fiction about past or future are you living in?

title: The appointment
body: An appointment with yourself is not a lesser kind than any other.
reflection: When did you last keep one?

title: On patience II
body: Everything in time. Nothing before it.
reflection: What is asking you to wait?

title: The hour of clarity
body: There is an hour each day when things are clearest. Worth finding and protecting.
reflection: What is your clearest hour?

title: On time passing
body: Time passes whether you use it well or not. This is either terrifying or motivating. Choose.
reflection: Which is it for you today?

title: On finitude
body: The fact that time ends is the thing that makes it matter.
reflection: Does knowing time ends change how you're using it?

title: On the hours
body: The hours are equal in length. They are not equal in quality.
reflection: What makes your best hours different from your worst?

title: On growing up
body: Every season of life prepares you for the next one, though rarely in ways you'd expect.
reflection: What is your current season preparing you for?

title: On use
body: Dost thou love life? Then do not squander time, for that is the stuff life is made of.
reflection: How are you using the stuff life is made of?
author: Benjamin Franklin

title: On urgency
body: The most important things are almost never the most urgent.
reflection: What is important but not urgent that you’ve been neglecting?

title: On the past
body: You can’t go back and change the beginning, but you can start where you are and change the ending.
reflection: What ending are you still able to change?
author: C.S. Lewis

title: On the present
body: The present moment always will have been.
reflection: What is happening right now that will always have been?

title: On the hours II
body: Lost time is never found again.
reflection: What time have you lost that you wish you hadn’t?
author: Benjamin Franklin

title: On eternity
body: The present moment is a gift, which is why it’s called the present.
reflection: What gift is this moment offering you?

title: On night
body: The night is more alive and more richly colored than the day.
reflection: What does the night bring that the day doesn’t?
author: Vincent van Gogh

title: On time and tide
body: Time and tide wait for no man.
reflection: What tide in your life are you at risk of missing?
author: Geoffrey Chaucer

title: On growing
body: We do not grow absolutely, chronologically. We grow sometimes in one dimension, and not in another.
reflection: In what dimension are you growing right now?
author: Anaïs Nin

title: On brevity
body: Life is short, art is long.
reflection: What would you create if you were more aware of that brevity?
author: Hippocrates

title: On living now
body: Realize deeply that the present moment is all you ever have.
reflection: What would change if you truly believed that?
author: Eckhart Tolle

title: On the years
body: Count your age by friends, not years. Count your life by smiles, not tears.
reflection: How old are you by that measure?
author: John Lennon

title: On dawns
body: Every day is a new beginning. Take a deep breath and start again.
reflection: What are you beginning again today?

title: On memory II
body: The real act of memory is an act of present-moment recognition.
reflection: What is this present moment asking you to recognize?

title: On aging
body: Age is an issue of mind over matter. If you don’t mind, it doesn’t matter.
reflection: What does your mind make of your age?
author: Mark Twain

title: On the long game
body: All great achievements require time.
reflection: What achievement are you playing the long game on?
author: Maya Angelou

title: On the long friendship with time
body: Learn to live with time, not against it.
reflection: Where are you currently fighting time?

title: On now
body: Be here now.
reflection: What is keeping you from being here?
author: Ram Dass

title: On ripeness
body: To everything there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven.
reflection: What season are you in, and what is its purpose?
author: Ecclesiastes 3:1

title: On irreversibility
body: Yesterday is gone. Tomorrow has not yet come. We have only today.
reflection: What does today ask of you?
author: Mother Teresa

title: On the clock
body: The bad news is time flies. The good news is you’re the pilot.
reflection: Where are you piloting yours?
author: Michael Altshuler

title: On the span
body: A man who dares to waste one hour of time has not discovered the value of life.
reflection: What is one hour of your time actually worth?
author: Charles Darwin

title: On later
body: One day or day one. You decide.
reflection: What are you saying one day to that could be day one?

title: On this hour
body: This moment contains all moments.
reflection: What does this moment contain?
author: C.S. Lewis

title: On impermanence
body: This too shall pass.
reflection: What are you hoping passes, and what are you hoping stays?

title: On the river
body: You cannot step into the same river twice.
reflection: What has changed in the river of your life?
author: Heraclitus

title: On the harvest
body: Plant seeds today. You won’t regret it.
reflection: What are you planting right now that you’ll harvest later?

title: On the good hour
body: One good hour a day, spent on what matters most, changes a life.
reflection: What would you spend your one good hour on?

title: On slowness
body: Slow is smooth. Smooth is fast.
reflection: Where would slowing down actually get you there faster?

title: On seasons passing
body: Every season gives you something the others don’t. Try not to rush through them.
reflection: What is this season giving you?

title: On duration II
body: A short story well told is worth more than a long one told poorly. Same goes for a life.
reflection: What chapter of your story are you telling right now?

title: On the good years
body: The good years aren’t always the easiest ones.
reflection: What year, in retrospect, was harder but better?

title: On waiting II
body: Patience is not the ability to wait. It is the ability to keep a good attitude while waiting.
reflection: What is your attitude while you wait?
author: Joyce Meyer

title: On continuity
body: Time is a flat circle. Everything we have done or will do, we will do over and over again.
reflection: What pattern in your life keeps returning?
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

title: On the new day
body: With the new day comes new strength and new thoughts.
reflection: What new thought does today bring?
author: Eleanor Roosevelt

title: What you own
body: Everything you own also owns a little of your attention.
reflection: What owns more of you than it should?

title: The simple meal
body: A simple meal, well made, with good company, is hard to improve on.
reflection: When did you last have one?

title: Fewer things
body: Fewer things, better chosen, is a principle that applies to more than possessions.
reflection: Where could you apply this principle in your life?

title: The one thing
body: If you could do one thing well today, what would it be?
reflection: What is that one thing?

title: Editing
body: The most useful skill is knowing what to remove.
reflection: What could you remove from your life right now?

title: Not adding
body: Progress is often not about adding but about stopping.
reflection: What could you stop?

title: The quiet life
body: A quiet life is not a small life. It is a life with the volume turned down on the right things.
reflection: What volume could you turn down?

title: Accumulation
body: Life accumulates things without asking whether they belong.
reflection: What has accumulated in your life that doesn’t belong?

title: Basic things
body: Basic things, done consistently, build more than complex things done occasionally.
reflection: What basic thing are you doing consistently?

title: Enough
body: Enough is a feeling, not a number. Once located, it changes everything.
reflection: What does enough feel like for you?

title: The one drawer
body: If you can’t find something, you have too much. Or you need one drawer.
reflection: What in your life is hard to find when you need it?

title: Cutting back
body: Cutting back on almost anything improves the quality of what remains.
reflection: What could you cut back on?

title: The uncomplicated day
body: An uncomplicated day is rarer than it should be and more valuable than it seems.
reflection: What would make tomorrow less complicated?

title: What you need
body: You need less than you think. This is both reassuring and inconvenient.
reflection: What could you do without that you’ve been treating as necessary?

title: The small house
body: A smaller space, well arranged, often serves better than a larger one, poorly.
reflection: What space in your life could be better arranged rather than expanded?

title: The ordinary object
body: A well-made ordinary object, used daily, is a quiet form of satisfaction.
reflection: What ordinary object in your life do you appreciate?

title: Decluttering
body: Decluttering is not about things. It’s about attention.
reflection: What has your attention been cluttered with?

title: The clear schedule
body: A day with some open space in it is a different kind of day.
reflection: When did you last have a day with open space?

title: Reducing
body: Most lists, schedules, and ambitions would benefit from a 30% reduction.
reflection: What is 30% too long in your life right now?

title: The simple task
body: A simple task done with full attention is more satisfying than a complex one done partially.
reflection: What simple task are you doing partially that deserves full attention?

title: Subtracting
body: Addition is easy. Subtraction is where the mastery is.
reflection: What could you subtract?

title: On wanting less
body: Wanting less is a skill. Like most skills, it gets easier with practice.
reflection: What do you want less of than you used to?

title: On commitments
body: Every commitment costs attention. The question is whether what you committed to is worth the price.
reflection: What commitment is costing you more than it’s giving?

title: The short list
body: A short list of what matters is more useful than a long one.
reflection: What’s on your short list?

title: Saying less
body: Saying less, more clearly, is a skill most people underestimate.
reflection: Where could you say less, more clearly?

title: Unscheduled
body: Unscheduled time is not empty time. It is potential time.
reflection: What potential does your unscheduled time hold?

title: The blank page
body: A blank page is either terrifying or liberating. Usually both, in sequence.
reflection: What blank page are you facing right now?

title: The uncomplicated person
body: Simple people, in the best sense, are easier to be around and harder to become.
reflection: What complicates you that you’d rather release?

title: One thing at a time
body: One thing at a time is not slow. It is actually the fastest way to get anything done well.
reflection: What are you multitasking that deserves your full attention?

title: The plain truth
body: The plain truth, plainly said, is almost always more useful than a complicated version.
reflection: What plain truth have you been complicating?

title: On ownership
body: You don’t own things. You manage them temporarily.
reflection: What are you managing that no longer deserves the effort?

title: The first principle
body: Most problems have a first principle underneath them. Finding it simplifies everything.
reflection: What first principle applies to your current biggest challenge?

title: Fewer words
body: Fewer words, better chosen, carry more.
reflection: Where could you say more with less?

title: The morning walk
body: A short walk in the morning clarifies more than most planning sessions.
reflection: When did you last take one?

title: On luxury
body: True luxury is not expensive. It is having exactly what you need and nothing you don’t.
reflection: What is your version of that?

title: The pared-down life
body: A pared-down life is not a diminished one. It is a more vivid one.
reflection: What would you pare down if you believed this?

title: On constraints
body: Constraints are not limitations. They are clarifiers.
reflection: What constraint is currently clarifying something for you?

title: The essentials
body: Strip away what isn’t essential and what remains tends to matter.
reflection: What is essential in your life right now?

title: On the simple life
body: The simple life is not simple to achieve. It takes deliberate choices made repeatedly.
reflection: What deliberate choice would simplify your life?

title: Slowing down
body: Slowing down is not falling behind. It is often getting ahead of the thing that matters.
reflection: What would you get ahead of by slowing down?

title: The one good thing
body: One good thing, done well, is a full day’s work.
reflection: What is your one good thing today?

title: On clarity
body: Clarity is the result of simplification.
reflection: What, simplified, would become clearer?

title: The white space
body: White space on a page makes the words more readable. White space in a life does the same.
reflection: Where could you add white space?

title: What you return to
body: What you return to again and again, without being told to, is probably what matters to you.
reflection: What do you keep returning to?

title: On minimalism
body: Minimalism is not about having nothing. It’s about making room for something.
reflection: What would you make room for?

title: The single priority
body: If everything is a priority, nothing is.
reflection: What is your actual priority right now?

title: On complication
body: Most problems are complicated not because they are hard but because they involve feelings.
reflection: What feeling is underneath your most complicated problem?

title: On the unadorned
body: Beauty unadorned is beauty most adorned.
reflection: Where in your life does simplicity produce beauty?

title: The quiet hour
body: A quiet hour is an investment in everything that follows it.
reflection: How do you invest your quiet hours?

title: On the simple answer
body: The simple answer is usually the right one. The complicated one is usually the defensive one.
reflection: What is the simple answer to your hardest question right now?

title: On reduction
body: Poetry is what is lost in translation. So is simplicity.
reflection: What have you translated into complexity that was simpler at the start?

title: The unbusy life
body: An unbusy life sounds boring until you have one.
reflection: What would an unbusy life make possible?

title: On doing one thing
body: The successful warrior is the average man, with laser-like focus.
reflection: What deserves your laser-like focus?
author: Bruce Lee

title: On less
body: Less is more.
reflection: Where would less give you more?
author: Robert Browning

title: On the nature of beauty
body: In character, in manner, in style, in all things, the supreme excellence is simplicity.
reflection: Where could you excel by simplifying?
author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow

title: On possessions
body: The things you own end up owning you.
reflection: What owns you that you’d like to be free of?
author: Chuck Palahniuk

title: On clutter
body: Have nothing in your house that you do not know to be useful, or believe to be beautiful.
reflection: What fails both tests?
author: William Morris

title: On order
body: For every minute spent organizing, an hour is earned.
reflection: What would be worth organizing?
author: Benjamin Franklin

title: On essentials
body: The ability to simplify means to eliminate the unnecessary so that the necessary may speak.
reflection: What is necessary that needs to speak louder in your life?
author: Hans Hofmann

title: On quiet
body: Simplicity involves unburdening your life, and living more lightly with fewer distractions.
reflection: What distraction could you put down?

title: On purity
body: Pure and simple, any truth is always simple.
reflection: What truth, simply stated, would clarify things for you?

title: On the small
body: Enjoy the little things, for one day you may look back and realize they were the big things.
reflection: What little thing is actually a big thing for you right now?
author: Robert Brault

title: On peace
body: Peace is the result of retraining your mind to process life as it is, rather than as you think it should be.
reflection: What difference between is and should be is costing you peace?
author: Wayne Dyer

title: On a single task
body: Work is hard. Distractions are plentiful. And time is short.
reflection: What work deserves your undistracted time?
author: Adam Hochschild

title: On contentment
body: Be content with what you have; rejoice in the way things are. When you realize there is nothing lacking, the whole world belongs to you.
reflection: What are you treating as lacking that isn’t?
author: Laozi

title: On focus
body: Concentrate all your thoughts upon the work at hand. The sun’s rays do not burn until brought to a focus.
reflection: What needs your focused rays?
author: Alexander Graham Bell

title: On the uncrowded life
body: Creativity is the residue of time wasted.
reflection: Are you giving yourself enough wasted time?
author: Albert Einstein

title: On being present
body: One reason so few of us achieve what we truly want is that we never direct our focus.
reflection: What are you not quite focusing on?
author: Tony Robbins

title: On the small beautiful
body: Beauty of style and harmony and grace and good rhythm depend on simplicity.
reflection: Where does simplicity produce grace in your life?
author: Plato

title: On reduction II
body: Any intelligent fool can make things bigger and more complex. It takes a touch of genius to move in the opposite direction.
reflection: Where could you move in the opposite direction?
author: E.F. Schumacher

title: On rest
body: Rest is not idleness, and to lie sometimes on the grass under the trees, listening to the murmur of water, is hardly a waste of time.
reflection: What rest have you been denying yourself?
author: John Lubbock

title: On pace
body: There is more to life than increasing its speed.
reflection: What are you speeding through that deserves more time?
author: Mahatma Gandhi

title: On the art of enough
body: The art of knowing when to stop is rarely taught.
reflection: When is enough enough for you?

title: On trimming
body: A poem is never finished, only abandoned. So is simplification—always one more thing you could trim.
reflection: What could you trim from your life right now?

title: On the unadorned life
body: Not knowing when to stop is the root of most excess.
reflection: Where have you not known when to stop?

title: On the daily
body: We are what we repeatedly do.
reflection: What are you repeatedly doing that you’d like to change?
author: Aristotle

title: On stripping away
body: I would have written a shorter letter, but I did not have the time.
reflection: What, given time, could you say more simply?
author: Blaise Pascal

title: On the single path
body: One step at a time is all it takes to get you there.
reflection: What is your next single step?

title: On depth over breadth
body: Rather than widening, try deepening.
reflection: Where would depth serve you better than breadth?

title: On the benefit of nothing
body: Sometimes the most productive thing you can do is relax.
reflection: What would you relax into if you let yourself?
author: Mark Black

title: On the gentle life
body: A gentle life is not a weak one. It is a deliberate one.
reflection: Where could more gentleness serve you?

title: On clearing the mind
body: Your mind is for having ideas, not holding them.
reflection: What ideas need to be put somewhere other than your mind?
author: David Allen

title: On the quiet mind
body: You don’t have to control your thoughts. You just have to stop letting them control you.
reflection: What thought is running you that you’d rather choose?
author: Dan Millman

title: On the present task
body: Wherever you are, be all there.
reflection: Where are you only partially?
author: Jim Elliot

title: On the obvious
body: The obvious is what no one notices until it’s pointed out.
reflection: What obvious thing in your life have you been overlooking?

title: On the slow path
body: Slow is the path to the places worth going.
reflection: What are you rushing toward that deserves a slower approach?

title: On the cost of complexity
body: Every added complexity is a hidden cost.
reflection: What hidden cost are you currently paying?

title: On the right size
body: Things should be as simple as possible, but not simpler.
reflection: What in your life is oversimplified in a way that causes problems?
author: Albert Einstein

title: On the virtue of less
body: It is preoccupation with possessions, more than anything else, that prevents us from living freely and nobly.
reflection: What preoccupation is preventing your freedom?
author: Bertrand Russell

title: On the plain path
body: Do what is right, not what is easy.
reflection: What is right that you’ve been avoiding because it isn’t easy?

title: On limits
body: Limits are not the enemy of creativity. They are often its source.
reflection: What creative thing might your limits be producing?

title: On the unadorned day
body: A day spent without urgency is a day that teaches you what you actually want.
reflection: What did your last unhurried day reveal?

title: On the light
body: In simplicity there is a kind of light that complexity covers up.
reflection: What light might simplifying reveal?

title: On the quiet center
body: At the center of every complicated situation is something surprisingly simple.
reflection: What is at the center of yours?

title: The craft
body: Craft is what you do when no one is watching and the deadline has passed.
reflection: What does your work look like when no one is watching?

title: Good work
body: Good work is its own reward. The other rewards are nice but not the point.
reflection: What work have you done recently that felt genuinely good?

title: What you make
body: What you make says something about what you believe.
reflection: What does your work say about what you believe?

title: The long career
body: A long career is built in weeks, not in moments of brilliance.
reflection: What are you building week by week?

title: Being useful
body: Being useful to others is one of the quieter satisfactions of a working life.
reflection: Who did your work serve today?

title: The unglamorous work
body: Most of the work that matters is unglamorous. That’s not a criticism of it.
reflection: What unglamorous work are you doing that matters?

title: Rest and output
body: What you produce when rested is almost always better than what you produce when depleted.
reflection: Are you rested enough to do your best work?

title: On starting
body: Starting is an act of faith. You don’t know if it will be any good until you’re in it.
reflection: What are you waiting to start?

title: The right tool
body: The right tool for the right task is a principle worth applying beyond carpentry.
reflection: What tool are you using that doesn’t quite fit?

title: What you’re for
body: Knowing what you’re for is more clarifying than knowing what you’re good at.
reflection: What are you for?

title: On collaboration
body: The best collaborative work usually happens when everyone feels safe enough to say the wrong thing.
reflection: How safe does your team feel to be wrong?

title: On learning
body: The willingness to be a beginner again is one of the most useful attitudes in a long career.
reflection: What are you a beginner at right now?

title: The habit of finishing
body: Finishing things is a habit. So is leaving them undone.
reflection: Which habit is stronger in you right now?

title: On boredom at work
body: Boredom at work is often a signal, not a failing.
reflection: What is your boredom signaling?

title: The mentor
body: A good mentor gives you something no course can: the experience of being seen in your work.
reflection: Who has seen you in your work?

title: Making something
body: There is a particular satisfaction in making something that didn’t exist before.
reflection: What have you made recently?

title: The wrong job
body: The wrong job teaches you what you actually want, which is not nothing.
reflection: What has a wrong fit taught you about what you actually want?

title: On reputation
body: Your reputation is built by what you do consistently, not what you do occasionally.
reflection: What are you doing consistently that builds the reputation you want?

title: On recognition
body: The work that gets recognized is rarely the work that mattered most.
reflection: What work of yours mattered that didn’t get recognized?

title: On quitting
body: Quitting the wrong thing is not failure. It’s navigation.
reflection: Is there something you should navigate away from?

title: Deep work
body: The deep work is where the value is. It is also what gets crowded out first.
reflection: How much deep work did you do this week?

title: The daily practice
body: A daily practice, even small, compounds into something remarkable.
reflection: What is your daily practice?

title: Purpose at work
body: Work without purpose is just motion. Motion without direction is exhausting.
reflection: What gives your work purpose?

title: On saying no at work
body: Every yes at work is a no to something else. The skill is in choosing well.
reflection: What did you say yes to recently that was actually a no to something more important?

title: On craft II
body: Craft is the difference between something that works and something that endures.
reflection: What in your work endures?

title: The good day
body: A good day at work rarely looks the way you planned it would.
reflection: What made your last good workday good?

title: On ambition
body: Ambition without direction becomes restlessness. Direction without ambition becomes complacency.
reflection: How are your ambition and direction aligned right now?

title: On failure
body: Failure is information. The only waste is failing to use it.
reflection: What information has a recent failure given you?

title: On the long career II
body: A career is not a sprint or a marathon. It’s more like farming—cycles of planting and harvesting.
reflection: What cycle are you in right now?

title: On slowing down at work
body: Working faster is rarely the answer to working better.
reflection: Where would slowing down improve your output?

title: The project that mattered
body: Not every project is worth the energy it costs. The ones that are change how you work.
reflection: What project changed how you work?

title: On visibility
body: Good work doesn’t always announce itself. Sometimes it requires a champion.
reflection: Whose work are you championing?

title: On courage at work
body: Saying the true thing in the room is an act of courage, even when it’s small.
reflection: What true thing have you not yet said?

title: On the second draft
body: First drafts are for getting it out. Second drafts are for making it good.
reflection: What is in first draft in your life right now?

title: The good enough
body: Perfect is the enemy of done. Done, imperfect, is often more useful than perfect, unfinished.
reflection: What are you polishing past the point of usefulness?

title: On working alone
body: Some of the best work happens alone, in quiet, without anyone watching.
reflection: What would you build if no one was watching?

title: On creative blocks
body: A creative block is usually a signal that something needs to be refilled before it can be expressed.
reflection: What needs refilling?

title: On expertise
body: Expertise is not knowing everything. It’s knowing where the edges are.
reflection: Where are the edges of your expertise?

title: On feedback
body: Feedback is a gift. Some gifts take time to appreciate.
reflection: What feedback are you still unwrapping?

title: On the hardest part
body: The hardest part of any project is usually the middle, where the beginning has worn off and the end isn’t yet visible.
reflection: Are you in a middle right now?

title: On meaning at work
body: Meaningful work doesn’t require a big title. It requires feeling that what you do matters.
reflection: Does what you do feel like it matters?

title: On transitions
body: Career transitions are disorienting and often the most generative thing that happens to you.
reflection: What transition are you in or approaching?

title: On maintenance
body: Most of work is maintenance. Maintenance is underrated.
reflection: What are you maintaining right now that deserves recognition?

title: On pride in work
body: Pride in your work is not the same as ego about it.
reflection: What work are you quietly proud of?

title: The overlooked skill
body: The most overlooked professional skill is knowing how to ask a good question.
reflection: What question should you be asking right now?

title: On endurance
body: Endurance is the most underrated professional virtue.
reflection: Where are you enduring right now?

title: On focus at work
body: A focused hour is worth more than an unfocused day.
reflection: When did you last have a truly focused hour?

title: On the work that stays
body: Some work you do for money. Some you do for love. The best work is when those overlap.
reflection: Where do money and love overlap in your work?

title: On rest as work
body: Rest is part of the work. Treating it as a reward for the work misses the point.
reflection: Are you resting enough to do the work well?

title: On showing up
body: Most of the work of a career is simply showing up, prepared, every day.
reflection: Are you showing up prepared?

title: On the long view at work
body: Careers are long. The decision that feels urgent today rarely determines the trajectory.
reflection: What decision are you treating as more urgent than it is?

title: On calling
body: A calling is not a job. It is the work you would do even if the money stopped.
reflection: Is your work a calling, a career, or a job right now?

title: On diligence
body: Genius is one percent inspiration, ninety-nine percent perspiration.
reflection: What are you perspiring toward?
author: Thomas Edison

title: On mastery
body: The master has failed more times than the beginner has even tried.
reflection: What have you failed at enough to be getting good at?
author: Stephen McCranie

title: On purpose
body: The two most important days in your life are the day you were born, and the day you find out why.
reflection: What is your why at work right now?
author: Mark Twain

title: On the long apprenticeship
body: Everyone starts as an amateur. The professional is just an amateur who didn’t stop.
reflection: What are you not stopping at?

title: On doing good work
body: Do the best you can until you know better. Then when you know better, do better.
reflection: What do you know better now that should change how you work?
author: Maya Angelou

title: On courage II
body: Whatever you can do or dream you can, begin it. Boldness has genius, power, and magic in it.
reflection: What bold beginning is available to you?
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe

title: On making
body: You can’t use up creativity. The more you use, the more you have.
reflection: What creative act have you been holding back?
author: Maya Angelou

title: On hard work
body: Hard work beats talent when talent doesn’t work hard.
reflection: Where are you relying on talent when hard work is what’s needed?
author: Tim Notke

title: On the work itself
body: Choose a job you love, and you will never have to work a day in your life.
reflection: How much of your work do you actually love?
author: Confucius

title: On originality
body: Good artists borrow. Great artists steal.
reflection: What have you borrowed that you’ve made your own?
author: Pablo Picasso

title: On output
body: You miss 100% of the shots you don’t take.
reflection: What shot haven’t you taken that you should?
author: Wayne Gretzky

title: On craft III
body: A professional is someone who can do his best work when he doesn’t feel like it.
reflection: When did you last do your best work when you didn’t feel like it?
author: Alistair Cooke

title: On the daily effort
body: Success is the sum of small efforts, repeated day in and day out.
reflection: What small effort are you repeating?
author: Robert Collier

title: On talent
body: Talent is cheaper than table salt. What separates the talented individual from the successful one is a lot of hard work.
reflection: Where is hard work separating you from where you were?
author: Stephen King

title: On the long horizon
body: Plant the seeds today that will give fruit in twenty years.
reflection: What are you planting for the long term?

title: On improvement
body: Kaizen: every day, a little better.
reflection: What is one small better thing you could do today?

title: On the team
body: If you want to go fast, go alone. If you want to go far, go together.
