reflection: Where do you need to go together?
author: African proverb

title: On the difficult work
body: The difficult we do immediately. The impossible takes a little longer.
reflection: What seemingly impossible thing are you working toward?

title: On clarity of purpose
body: If you don’t know where you’re going, any road will get you there.
reflection: Do you know where you’re going?
author: Lewis Carroll

title: On the process
body: Fall in love with the process and the results will come.
reflection: What process are you learning to love?
author: Eric Thomas

title: On the good problem
body: A problem well stated is a problem half solved.
reflection: What problem needs better stating?
author: Charles Kettering

title: On the long build
body: Rome wasn’t built in a day.
reflection: What are you building that deserves the long patience?

title: On the work ethic
body: The only place where success comes before work is in the dictionary.
reflection: What work are you putting in right now?
author: Vidal Sassoon

title: On resilience
body: Fall seven times, stand up eight.
reflection: How many times have you stood back up?
author: Japanese proverb

title: On depth
body: Be so good they can’t ignore you.
reflection: What would being that good look like in your field?
author: Steve Martin

title: On originality II
body: Everything has been done before. Your particular combination of influences hasn’t.
reflection: What is your particular combination?

title: On revision
body: Writing without revision is thinking without reconsideration.
reflection: What in your work deserves reconsideration?

title: On the unfinished project
body: An abandoned project teaches you where your commitment ends.
reflection: What abandoned project has something still to teach you?

title: On the senior years
body: The best work of a career often comes toward the end, when fear has been replaced by confidence.
reflection: What work could you only do now, with everything you’ve learned?

title: On the question
body: The right question is more valuable than the right answer.
reflection: What is the right question for your work right now?

title: On constraints II
body: The most creative solutions almost always come from the tightest constraints.
reflection: What constraint is forcing your creativity?

title: On showing your work
body: Showing your work matters more than protecting it.
reflection: What are you protecting that might benefit from being shared?

title: What stays
body: The relationships that stay through hard times are the ones worth most.
reflection: Who has stayed through your hard times?

title: The hard conversation
body: The hard conversation postponed becomes harder. There’s rarely a good reason to wait.
reflection: What conversation are you postponing?

title: Being seen
body: Being truly seen by someone is one of the rarest and most valuable things.
reflection: Who sees you? Do they know that’s what they do for you?

title: What you offer
body: What you offer people in a relationship matters less than how you make them feel.
reflection: How do the people closest to you feel around you?

title: On showing up
body: Showing up consistently is the foundation of any relationship worth having.
reflection: Where are you showing up consistently?

title: The old friend
body: An old friend holds a version of you that the new people in your life haven’t met.
reflection: When did you last spend time with someone who knew you then?

title: On forgiveness
body: Forgiveness is not a gift to the other person. It’s a gift to yourself.
reflection: Who do you need to forgive for your own sake?

title: On listening II
body: Listening is not waiting for your turn to speak.
reflection: When did you last listen without preparing your response?

title: The long marriage
body: A long marriage is not a single relationship. It’s many relationships, one after another, with the same person.
reflection: What version of your closest relationship are you in now?

title: On distance
body: Distance in a relationship isn’t always about geography.
reflection: Where is there distance in a relationship that matters to you?

title: On caring
body: Caring is shown more in time spent than in words offered.
reflection: Who deserves more of your time right now?

title: On arguments
body: Most arguments are not about the thing they appear to be about.
reflection: What is your most recent conflict actually about?

title: The apology
body: A genuine apology changes something between two people. A performed one changes nothing.
reflection: Is there an apology you owe that would be genuine?

title: On gratitude in relationships
body: Thanking the people who matter to you costs nothing and means a great deal.
reflection: Who deserves thanks from you that hasn’t received it?

title: The slow drift
body: Relationships drift apart slowly, then suddenly. The gradual part is where it can be stopped.
reflection: Is there a relationship drifting that you could stop?

title: On presence in relationships
body: Being physically present and being actually present are not the same thing.
reflection: Where are you present in body but absent in attention?

title: On the family you choose
body: The family you choose can be as sustaining as the one you were born into.
reflection: Who is your chosen family?

title: The unexpected friend
body: The best friendships are often the ones you didn’t plan on.
reflection: Who arrived in your life unexpectedly and stayed?

title: On trust in relationships
body: Trust is built in small moments and lost in large ones. The math is unfair but reliable.
reflection: What small moments are building trust in your relationships?

title: On letting go
body: Some relationships run their course. Letting them end with grace is its own kind of love.
reflection: Is there a relationship you’re holding past its natural end?

title: Being known
body: To be known by someone is one of the deepest things you can give or receive.
reflection: Who knows you? Who do you know?

title: On vulnerability
body: Vulnerability is not weakness. It is the entry point to connection.
reflection: Where have you been vulnerable recently, and what did it open up?

title: On giving
body: In relationships, the one who gives most tends to receive most. Not immediately. But eventually.
reflection: Are you giving or withholding right now?

title: On the phone call
body: A phone call instead of a text is a different kind of reaching out. Still worth doing.
reflection: Who would benefit from a call rather than a message?

title: The neighbor
body: The people nearest you geographically are often the least known.
reflection: How well do you know your neighbors?

title: On respect
body: Respect in a relationship outlasts almost everything else.
reflection: Do your closest relationships have it?

title: On being difficult
body: Everyone is difficult sometimes. The question is whether you’re worth the difficulty.
reflection: Are you worth the difficulty you bring right now?

title: On kindness in relationships
body: Kindness in an established relationship is easy to forget. Worth remembering.
reflection: When did you last do something kind for someone you take for granted?

title: The check-in
body: Checking in on someone—genuinely asking how they are—is an act of love.
reflection: Who should you check in on?

title: On differences
body: The places where two people are most different are often where they teach each other most.
reflection: What have you learned from someone very different from you?

title: On history
body: Shared history is the substrate of intimacy.
reflection: What history do you share with the people closest to you?

title: The relationship audit
body: It is worth asking periodically which relationships give energy and which drain it.
reflection: What does that audit reveal right now?

title: On new people
body: Meeting new people at any age expands what’s possible.
reflection: Who have you met recently who has expanded something for you?

title: On investment
body: Relationships require investment. The return is rarely immediate and usually substantial.
reflection: Where are you investing in a relationship right now?

title: On the parent
body: The relationship with a parent—or the absence of one—shapes almost everything.
reflection: What does your relationship with your parent(s) still teach you about yourself?

title: On siblings
body: Siblings carry a shared history no one else has. That is worth something, even when it’s complicated.
reflection: What does the relationship with your sibling(s) mean to you now?

title: The unspoken thing
body: Every close relationship has unspoken things. Some of them need to be said.
reflection: What is unspoken in a relationship that might need saying?

title: On admiration
body: Telling someone what you admire about them is a gift most people forget to give.
reflection: Who do you admire that you’ve never told?

title: On time together
body: Time spent together without agenda is where relationships deepen.
reflection: When did you last spend time like that with someone you care about?

title: On repair
body: The ability to repair a rupture in a relationship is more important than the ability to avoid one.
reflection: What relationship rupture have you repaired, or still needs repair?

title: On asking
body: Asking people about themselves, and truly listening, is a form of generosity.
reflection: Who could use your genuine curiosity right now?

title: On the meal together
body: Sharing a meal is one of the oldest forms of building relationship.
reflection: Who would you like to share a meal with soon?

title: On memory in relationships
body: What you remember about people says something about what you value in them.
reflection: What do you most remember about the people closest to you?

title: On the letter
body: A written letter is one of the more lasting things you can give someone.
reflection: Who deserves a letter from you?

title: On disagreement
body: Disagreement in a healthy relationship is normal. Contempt is not.
reflection: Is there contempt in any of your relationships that needs to be addressed?

title: On reunion
body: A reunion with someone you love, after time apart, is one of the sweetest things.
reflection: Who are you most looking forward to seeing?

title: On the long conversation
body: The best conversations happen over many years, returning to the same themes in different lights.
reflection: What ongoing conversation defines your closest relationship?

title: On mentorship
body: Being mentored changes your trajectory. Being a mentor changes your character.
reflection: Who are you mentoring, or who should you be?

title: On consistency
body: Showing up consistently, even when it’s inconvenient, is the measure of a relationship.
reflection: Where are you showing up consistently for someone?

title: On the long goodbye
body: Some goodbyes are too gradual to notice until they’re complete.
reflection: Is there a goodbye happening in your life right now?

title: On acceptance
body: Accepting someone as they are—not as you wish they were—is love’s hardest practice.
reflection: Who are you struggling to accept as they are?

title: On boundaries
body: A boundary is not a wall. It is a door with a handle on the inside.
reflection: What boundary would protect a relationship you value?

title: On asking for what you need
body: Asking for what you need is not a burden on others. It is an invitation to connection.
reflection: What do you need from someone close to you that you haven’t asked for?

title: On the child
body: The relationship with a child teaches you things about love you couldn’t learn any other way.
reflection: What have children in your life taught you about love?

title: On reaching out
body: Most people are glad when you reach out. The risk of doing it is almost always smaller than it seems.
reflection: Who have you been meaning to reach out to?

title: On love and time
body: We are most ourselves in the presence of people who have known us the longest.
reflection: Who brings out the most settled version of you?

title: On grief together
body: Grieving together is one of the most intimate things people can do.
reflection: Who have you grieved with?

title: On the long love
body: Love that has lasted is a different thing than love that is new. Both are real.
reflection: What is your oldest love teaching you right now?

title: On what lasts
body: What lasts in a relationship is rarely what you think it will be at the beginning.
reflection: What has lasted that surprised you?

title: On connection
body: The deepest human need is to be known and loved anyway.
reflection: Where do you experience that?

title: On love
body: To love and be loved is to feel the sun from both sides.
reflection: Where are you feeling the sun from both sides?
author: David Viscott

title: On friendship
body: A friend is someone who knows all about you and still loves you.
reflection: Who is that for you?
author: Elbert Hubbard

title: On presence
body: The greatest gift you can give someone is your time, because when you give your time you are giving a portion of your life.
reflection: Who deserves more of that gift from you?
author: Unknown

title: On the long bond
body: We are all leaves of one tree. We are all waves of one sea.
reflection: Who do you feel most bonded to?
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

title: On being there
body: In the end, we will remember not the words of our enemies, but the silence of our friends.
reflection: Where have you been silent when someone needed you?
author: Martin Luther King, Jr.

title: On kindness
body: Be kind, for everyone you meet is fighting a battle you know nothing about.
reflection: Who in your life might be fighting something you can’t see?
author: Wendy Mass

title: On love II
body: The best thing to hold onto in life is each other.
reflection: Who are you holding onto?
author: Audrey Hepburn

title: On what endures
body: At the end of life, what we regret most are things we didn’t say or do for the people we love.
reflection: Is there something unsaid or undone that you could offer now?

title: On generosity
body: Give what you have. To someone, it may be better than you dare to think.
reflection: What do you have that someone in your life needs?
author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow

title: On the imperfect relationship
body: The most important thing in the world is to love someone. The second most important thing is to have someone to love you.
reflection: Do you have both?
author: James Thurber

title: On closeness
body: The meeting of two personalities is like the contact of two chemical substances: if there is any reaction, both are transformed.
reflection: Who has transformed you?
author: Carl Jung

title: On showing love
body: Not all those who wander are lost. Not all who are quiet are unloving.
reflection: Who shows love in a way you almost missed?
author: J.R.R. Tolkien

title: On being known II
body: You don’t love someone for their looks, or their clothes, or their fancy car, but because they sing a song only you can hear.
reflection: Whose song do you hear?
author: Unknown

title: On loyalty
body: The greatest healing therapy is friendship and love.
reflection: Who heals you just by being present?
author: Hubert Humphrey

title: On deepening
body: Deep roots are not reached by the frost.
reflection: Which of your relationships has the deepest roots?
author: J.R.R. Tolkien

title: On tenderness
body: There is no exercise better for the heart than reaching down and lifting people up.
reflection: Who could use being lifted right now?
author: John Holmes

title: On truth in love
body: The truth is rarely pure and never simple, but love makes it bearable.
reflection: What truth in a relationship is love helping you bear?
author: Oscar Wilde

title: On the ordinary love
body: The most ordinary moments with the right person become extraordinary in memory.
reflection: What ordinary moment with someone you love do you want to remember?

title: On showing up II
body: Love is not what you say. Love is what you do.
reflection: What are you doing to show love right now?

title: The morning light
body: Morning light arrives differently each day. It is worth stopping to see what kind it is.
reflection: What did this morning’s light look like?

title: The season’s end
body: Every season ends in a way that prepares the next.
reflection: What is ending right now that is preparing something new?

title: Rain
body: Rain asks nothing of you except to be inside and unhurried.
reflection: When did you last let rain be company?

title: The garden
body: A garden teaches patience and acceptance in equal measure.
reflection: What is your garden, literal or otherwise?

title: After a storm
body: After a storm the air is different. Cleaner. Worth noticing.
reflection: What storm has cleared the air recently?

title: The tree
body: A very old tree has outlived most of what seemed permanent.
reflection: What in your life feels permanent that won’t be?

title: Walking outside
body: Walking outside changes what you think about. You leave with different questions than you arrived with.
reflection: What question do you want to walk with?

title: Winter
body: Winter is not an absence of life. It is life at rest.
reflection: What in your life is at rest, waiting for spring?

title: The first warm day
body: The first warm day of spring is a reminder that everything comes back eventually.
reflection: What have you been waiting for to return?

title: The ocean
body: The ocean has been indifferent to human urgency since long before us and will be long after.
reflection: What urgent thing, seen from that scale, shrinks a little?

title: The forest
body: In a forest, everything is connected to everything else in ways you can’t see.
reflection: What connection in your life are you not seeing?

title: Sunset
body: A sunset is worth stopping for. You already knew that. When did you last stop?
reflection: When did you last stop for a sunset?

title: The stars
body: The stars have been there every night. The clouds just sometimes get in the way.
reflection: What constant in your life have the clouds been obscuring?

title: The river
body: A river finds its way without knowing the destination.
reflection: Where are you trusting the flow?

title: Autumn
body: Autumn is the season that teaches letting go most beautifully.
reflection: What are you releasing this autumn of your life?

title: The bird’s song
body: A bird’s song at dawn doesn’t mean anything. It just is. That’s enough.
reflection: What in your life is enough, just by being?

title: The path
body: Most paths through nature were made by something that simply kept going the same way.
reflection: What path are you making by simply continuing?

title: The mountain
body: A mountain looks insurmountable from the bottom and manageable once you’re on it.
reflection: What mountain looks smaller than it did?

title: Spring
body: Spring arrives on its own schedule, regardless of how ready you are.
reflection: What arrival in your life is on its own schedule?

title: The tide
body: The tide goes out and comes back. Neither state is permanent.
reflection: What tide in your life is on its way back?

title: Fog
body: Fog doesn’t last. It just makes everything harder to see until it lifts.
reflection: What fog in your life is likely to lift?

title: The lake
body: Still water reflects more accurately than moving water.
reflection: When are you still enough to reflect clearly?

title: Summer
body: Summer has a particular quality of abundance that the other seasons don’t.
reflection: What is abundant in your life right now?

title: The harvest
body: You harvest what you plant. Sometimes late. Always eventually.
reflection: What are you harvesting now that you planted years ago?

title: The seed
body: A seed contains everything it will become, in a form unrecognizable from the result.
reflection: What are you in seed form right now?

title: The field
body: A field at rest is not a wasted field. It is a field preparing.
reflection: What in you is resting and preparing?

title: Snow
body: Snow covers everything equally. There is something restoring about that.
reflection: What in your life could use a fresh covering?

title: The wind
body: Wind moves through without staying. It doesn’t need to stay to have an effect.
reflection: What has passed through your life and left its mark?

title: The shore
body: Standing at the shore you are at the edge of something much larger than your concerns.
reflection: What edge are you standing at?

title: The meadow
body: A meadow in summer is doing nothing and producing everything.
reflection: What are you producing by doing less?

title: The old growth
body: Old growth forest has layers of life that a young forest hasn’t had time to develop.
reflection: What layers has time added to your life?

title: The sky
body: The sky is always changing and always there. Both things are true.
reflection: What in your life is always changing and always there?

title: The valley
body: A valley is surrounded on all sides. It is also sheltered.
reflection: What in your current situation, while limiting, also shelters you?

title: Dawn
body: Dawn comes before you have to do anything. It belongs to no one’s agenda.
reflection: What would you do with a truly agenda-free morning?

title: The moon
body: The moon follows its own rhythm regardless of what’s happening below.
reflection: What rhythm would you follow if you weren’t following the world’s?

title: The root system
body: What keeps a tree standing is invisible.
reflection: What invisible thing keeps you standing?

title: The turning leaves
body: Leaves turn their most brilliant just before falling. There is a lesson in that.
reflection: What in your life is most vivid just before its end?

title: The frost
body: Frost arrives without asking permission and changes everything by morning.
reflection: What has arrived in your life without warning and changed things?

title: The creek
body: A small creek finds its way around every obstacle without effort or urgency.
reflection: Where could you find your way around rather than through?

title: The night sky
body: The night sky puts things in perspective in a way nothing made by humans can.
reflection: When did you last look at it?

title: The seasons of a life
body: A life has seasons too. The winter ones teach what the summer ones cannot.
reflection: What is your current season teaching you?

title: The still morning
body: A still morning before the day begins is one of the world’s most reliable gifts.
reflection: Are you collecting that gift?

title: The cliff edge
body: From a cliff edge you see what you can’t from lower ground.
reflection: What higher vantage point would change what you see?

title: The season of growth
body: Growth is quieter and slower than it appears. It happens while you’re doing other things.
reflection: What growth is happening in you right now that you haven’t noticed?

title: The long winter
body: A long winter tests your belief that spring is coming. It always has been.
reflection: What long winter are you in, and what is your spring?

title: The warming earth
body: The earth warms slowly in spring, not all at once. So does most healing.
reflection: What slow warming is happening in you?

title: On the earth
body: The earth does not belong to us. We belong to the earth.
reflection: How are you inhabiting the earth as a guest?
author: Chief Seattle

title: On trees
body: A nation that destroys its soils destroys itself. Forests are the lungs of our land.
reflection: What sustains you that you haven’t been protecting?
author: Franklin D. Roosevelt

title: On the sea
body: The sea, once it casts its spell, holds one in its net of wonder forever.
reflection: What wonder has cast its spell on you?
author: Jacques Cousteau

title: On the sky
body: The sky is not the limit. It is just the beginning.
reflection: What feels like a limit that might actually be a beginning?

title: On the river II
body: A river cuts through rock not because of its power, but its persistence.
reflection: What are you persisting at that is slowly cutting through?
author: Jim Watkins

title: On the forest II
body: One must ask children and birds how cherries and strawberries taste.
reflection: What would a child notice about nature that you’ve stopped noticing?
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe

title: On the garden II
body: To plant a garden is to believe in tomorrow.
reflection: What are you planting that expresses your belief in tomorrow?
author: Audrey Hepburn

title: On wildness
body: In wildness is the preservation of the world.
reflection: Where do you encounter wildness in your life?
author: Henry David Thoreau

title: On the dawn
body: Every day is a new beginning. The dawn makes that literal.
reflection: What new beginning is available at this dawn?

title: On water
body: Water is the softest thing, yet it can penetrate mountains and earth. This shows clearly the principle of softness overcoming hardness.
reflection: Where could softness serve you better than force?
author: Laozi

title: On observation
body: Look deep into nature, and then you will understand everything better.
reflection: What have you understood better by looking at the natural world?
author: Albert Einstein

title: On the living world
body: The poetry of the earth is never dead.
reflection: What poetry have you noticed in the earth around you?
author: John Keats

title: On the small
body: Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they neither toil nor spin, yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.
reflection: What simple beauty is available to you right now, without effort?
author: Matthew 6:28-29

title: On the earth II
body: He that plants trees loves others beside himself.
reflection: What are you doing for those who come after you?
author: Thomas Fuller

title: On the horizon
body: I love the sea’s sounds and the way it reflects the sky. The colors are extraordinary.
reflection: What extraordinary color or light have you noticed lately?
author: John Dillard

title: On the still small things
body: It is not the mountain we conquer, but ourselves.
reflection: What are you conquering right now?
author: Edmund Hillary

title: On grounding
body: Forget not that the earth delights to feel your bare feet and the winds long to play with your hair.
reflection: When did you last let the earth delight in you?
author: Khalil Gibran

title: On renewal
body: In the spring, at the end of the day, you should smell like dirt.
reflection: What have you been getting your hands into?
author: Margaret Atwood

title: On belonging to nature
body: The world is not a problem to be solved; it is a living being to which we belong.
reflection: How are you belonging to the living world?
author: Llewelyn Vaughan-Lee

title: On the evening
body: I have watched the sun set over many mountains and oceans, and I have seen the way it changes everything.
reflection: What does a setting sun change in you?

title: On frost and warmth
body: No winter lasts forever; no spring skips its turn.
reflection: What spring are you waiting for?
author: Hal Borland

title: On the quiet earth
body: There is a pleasure in the pathless woods, there is a rapture on the lonely shore.
reflection: Where do you find rapture in solitude with nature?
author: Lord Byron

title: On the world’s gifts
body: The clearest way into the Universe is through a forest wilderness.
reflection: What wilderness clears your view?
author: John Muir

title: On the seasons of the heart
body: Even the seasons form a great circle in their changing, and always come back again to where they were.
reflection: What circle in your life keeps returning?
author: Black Elk

title: On tides
body: The ocean stirs the heart, inspires the imagination, and brings eternal joy to the soul.
reflection: What stirs your heart right now?
author: Robert Wyland

title: On the earth’s patience
body: Earth laughs in flowers.
reflection: What is your earth laughing at right now?
author: Ralph Waldo Emerson

title: On the wind
body: Like a great wind, life is all around us, and we must move with it or be moved by it.
reflection: Where are you moving with life rather than against it?

title: On the dawn II
body: Morning is when I am awake and there is a dawn in me.
reflection: What awakens in you at your best hours?
author: Henry David Thoreau

title: On what remains
body: The mountains will always be there. The trick is to make sure you are too.
reflection: What are you doing to ensure you’re here for the mountains?

title: On autumn II
body: Delicious autumn! My very soul is wedded to it, and if I were a bird I would fly about the earth seeking the successive autumns.
reflection: What would you seek in the successive autumns of your life?
author: George Eliot

title: On wonder
body: The world is full of magic things, patiently waiting for our senses to grow sharper.
reflection: What magic thing have you recently noticed?
author: W.B. Yeats

title: On the small garden
body: The best place to find God is in a garden. You can dig for him there.
reflection: Where do you find what you’re looking for?
author: George Bernard Shaw

title: On listening to nature
body: If you listen carefully, the silence of the forest will speak to you.
reflection: What has the natural world been saying to you?

title: On what nature teaches
body: Study nature, love nature, stay close to nature. It will never fail you.
reflection: What has nature taught you lately?
author: Frank Lloyd Wright

title: On abundance
body: The earth has enough for everyone’s need, but not for everyone’s greed.
reflection: Where are you taking more than you need?
author: Mahatma Gandhi

title: On the changing world
body: The same stream of life that runs through my veins night and day runs through the world and dances in rhythmic measure.
reflection: What rhythm are you moving to right now?
author: Rabindranath Tagore

title: On shelter
body: A house is not a home unless it contains food and fire for the mind as well as the body.
reflection: What feeds your mind in your home?
author: Benjamin Franklin

title: On rain II
body: Let the rain kiss you. Let the rain beat upon your head with silver liquid drops. Let the rain sing you a lullaby.
reflection: When did you last let something wash over you?
author: Langston Hughes

title: On the living moment
body: Look at a tree, a flower, a plant. Let your awareness rest upon it. How still they are, how deeply rooted in Being.
reflection: What stillness is available to you right now?
author: Eckhart Tolle

title: On the forest floor
body: A walk in the woods feeds the soul.
reflection: What feeds your soul that takes you outside?

title: On simple gifts
body: The best things in life aren’t things.
reflection: What best thing in your life is not a thing?
author: Art Buchwald

title: The ultimate measure
body: The ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in moments of comfort and convenience, but where he stands at times of challenge and controversy.
reflection: How do you respond when the path becomes difficult?
author: Martin Luther King, Jr.
